title: Why Job Fit Is Important in Hiring and How To Do a Job Fit Assessment
text:
Hiring Resources

# Why Job Fit Is Important in Hiring and How To Do a Job Fit Assessment

Raji Oluwaniyi

By Raji Oluwaniyi

Job fit assessment

For many recruiters, the definition of a mishire is a new employee who doesn’t have the skills to do the job. While this is correct to an extent, it only covers the surface of the issue, leaving factors like job fit and culture add out of the picture. Ideally, the perfect candidate should possess the skills, personality, and values to fit into the role easily, beyond just doing the job right. And the only way to evaluate this properly is with a job fit assessment.

However, in businesses today, you’d find that many recruiters still struggle with hiring the right candidates for the role. Instead, they end up with employees who can do the job but don’t fit the role enough to excel in it. If you’re experiencing this, you’re in the right place. This article explains why job fit is so important and how to conduct a job fit assessment effectively.

## What is job fit?

Job fit is a measure of how well a candidate can adapt to a role and excel in it. Candidates with great job fit not only have the ability to succeed in a role, but they also have immense potential to bond with colleagues and solve problems in the work environment.

Job fit also refers to how well a person's skills (hard and soft skills), values, and personality fit into a role and the company at large. It also defines how motivated the candidate is for the role, as well as how driven they are to actually achieve the company’s goals while working in the position.

Unlike skills mismatches, job fit addresses the overall qualities and capabilities required for a role. This means that a skills mismatch is an example of poor job fit, but poor job fit doesn’t always mean the candidate lacks the skills required for the role. Here are some examples of scenarios where candidates have poor job fit:

* An excellent coder who cannot collaborate effectively with the rest of the team.
* A highly collaborative teammate who lacks the hard skills required to write effective code.
* A member of the leadership team whose values don’t align with the values of the wider business.

Overall, job fit is a key determinant of overall performance and even success in a role. This means that if you hire candidates with great job fit, they’re more likely to thrive with your company and work to help you achieve your goals.

## Why is job fit important?

“Swap for Another” option

Primarily, job fit is important to ensure the best hires. After all, you can’t say a candidate is the best fit for the job if they don’t possess the all-round capacity to function maximally in the role. In fact, it’s safe to say that the actual importance of job fit is reflected significantly after the hiring process.

On one hand, a candidate with poor job fit can be lacking in at least one aspect of the requirements for the role. When hired, even though they have the motivation for the job, it can dwindle due to the constant feeling of falling behind their colleagues.

Additionally, these new employees tend to slow down typical company processes. For example, by delivering late on tasks, submitting half-done work, or falling behind with collaboration, they can prevent others from doing their jobs, thus slowing down overall productivity.

From a different angle, a candidate with poor job fit can also be one who possesses qualities fit for a role above the open one. For example, an engineer may have the perfect technical background for a role in the oil and gas industry. But if their values do not match those of their employer, they will soon become disengaged and begin to seek employment elsewhere.

In essence, the imbalance from poor job fit either causes the new hires to feel over- or underchallenged enough in the role. Over time, it can result in increased employee turnover, which damages the company’s productivity and attracts extra costs to rehire and close the skill gap.

## What is a job fit assessment?

Put simply, a job fit assessment is a standardized process where recruiters or hiring managers evaluate how fit a candidate is for a role. This includes checking for:

* Soft skills
* Technical skills
* Personality traits
* Work ethics and values
* Cognitive ability
* Motivations
* Culture fit and culture add

Comprehensive job fit assessments are a combination of skill-based job descriptions, skills tests, and structured interviews in any order or frequency, depending on the role and your company’s needs.

## Benefits of a job fit assessment

Skill gaps are at an all-time high, with about 85% of hiring managers today reporting a skills gap in their organization that needs to be addressed. However, according to Gallup, the cost of replacing an employee in the US can range from one-half to two times their annual salary and costs US businesses one trillion dollars per year in total. This financial impact can take a toll on the company's planning and budget.

Besides the financial impact, high turnover can lead to low morale, overworked employees, lower productivity, and a slip in work quality. But with job fit assessments, you can avoid these and ensure your company matches the best candidate to the open role. Let’s have a look at the benefits of adopting a job fit assessment in your recruitment:

### 1\. Improved employee performance

When candidates are well-matched to the tasks they are responsible for, they are able to work more efficiently and effectively, completing assignments with higher accuracy and consistency. This alignment increases their confidence and reduces errors, as employees understand their responsibilities and are equipped to handle them.

Over time, it translates into measurable improvements in productivity, quality of work, and overall organizational performance. By identifying the right fit from the outset, companies create an environment in which employees can perform to their full potential.

### 2\. Reduced employee turnover

Job fit assessments contribute significantly to employee retention by ensuring alignment between individual capabilities, work preferences, and organizational culture. Employees who feel suited to their roles and comfortable within their teams are less likely to experience dissatisfaction, stress, or disengagement.

This reduces the risk of premature resignation and the associated costs of recruitment, training, and onboarding. Retaining skilled employees enhances continuity, fosters institutional knowledge, and strengthens team cohesion, which benefits both colleagues and the organization.

### 3\. Improved recruitment efficiency

Integrating job fit assessments into the hiring process allows organizations to evaluate candidates objectively and thoroughly. By examining skills, cognitive abilities, personality traits, and potential for success, you can identify applicants who are most likely to excel in the role.

This targeted approach reduces the time to hire, prevents you from spending resources on underqualified candidates, and ensures better hiring decisions. Additionally, new hires selected through fit assessments typically require less supervision and adapt more quickly, resulting in faster onboarding and earlier contributions to organizational goals.

### 4\. It supports career development and succession planning

Job fit assessments provide valuable insights into your employees’ capabilities, potential, and readiness for greater responsibilities. By understanding strengths and developmental needs, your company can plan career progression, identify candidates for promotion, and prepare employees for succession into critical roles.

The result is that talent is developed strategically, minimizing skill gaps and maintaining operational continuity. Employees also benefit from clear guidance, opportunities for growth, and alignment with roles where they can succeed, which fosters loyalty and engagement. At the end of the day, your business can build a pipeline of capable talent, enhancing leadership readiness and ensuring sustainable development.

## How to do a job fit assessment

Code Editor

There is a danger that organizations seeking a “unicorn” candidate that ticks every box of the job requirements will limit their chances of making a good hire by setting the bar too high. Instead of adopting this high-expectations approach, a better method is to be alert for red flags and conduct a thorough job fit assessment to compare candidates and locate the best person for the job. Here’s how to conduct a job fit assessment test successfully:

### Step 1: Define the requirements for the open role

Before you can properly assess how fit a candidate is, you must understand what it would take to be fit for the job. Doing this means determining the soft and hard skills the candidate must have, as well as the cognitive ability, work ethic, personality, and value alignment they should possess.

Doing this involves performing a comprehensive job analysis beforehand, which would help identify key responsibilities, performance expectations, and potential challenges an employee will face. It’s also important to consider the organizational culture, team dynamics, and long-term career path the role may offer.

### Step 2. Develop a job description for the role

Next, based on your research, you can develop a job description for the position. This ensures that candidate expectations are realistic and aligned with organizational objectives from the start.

A recent study by G2 showed that 73% of job seekers won’t apply for a role unless the company’s culture aligns with their own. As such, while writing your job description, it must paint a detailed picture of your company’s values and skills alongside the required skills for the role. This ensures a higher quality of candidates applying for the role and prevents those who don’t resonate with your company’s culture from applying.

### Step 3. Assess core skills and competencies

Once the role is well-defined, the next step is to evaluate whether the candidate possesses the technical skills and competencies required. This involves reviewing qualifications, previous work experience, or conducting practical skill tests.

For example, a finance role may demand strong analytical ability and attention to detail, while a customer service role may require communication and problem-solving skills.

Beyond technical expertise, assessing transferable skills such as adaptability, teamwork, and leadership potential is equally important. Structured interviews and skill assessments help provide measurable insights into these areas so you can make better hiring decisions. This way, you can hire employees who not only meet immediate needs but can also adapt and grow with the position over time.

### Step 4. Evaluate personality and behavioral traits

Assessing for job fit goes beyond evaluating candidates’ technical skills alone. You’d have to measure personality and behavior as they determine how employees would perform within an organization.

To do this, you can apply a combination of psychometric tests and behavioral interviews to reveal traits like resilience, motivation, decision-making style, and communication preferences. For example, a highly independent personality may thrive in roles requiring autonomy, while people with collaborative traits would thrive in team-based positions.

Evaluating behavior also helps predict how individuals will handle stress, resolve conflicts, and interact with colleagues. These assessments help reduce the risk of mismatched expectations and ensure employees can integrate seamlessly into existing teams and company culture.

### Step 5. Compare candidate strengths with organizational culture

Once you’ve clearly defined and assessed the essential skills for the position, the next step is to compare them directly with each candidate’s capabilities based on the results of your assessments. A practical way to do this is by creating a skills matrix, where you list job requirements alongside the candidate’s demonstrated abilities. You can also use rating scales like beginner, intermediate, or advanced to measure proficiency levels objectively.

To make the process even more seamless, you can adopt a platform like Vervoe, which provides candidate scorecards that break down skills and competencies in detail. These scorecards allow recruiters to see at a glance how each person measures up to the role requirements, making side-by-side comparisons more accurate and efficient.

At the end of this process, you can bring together all the insights about skills, competencies, personality traits, and cultural alignment to make an informed decision. When done properly, the outcome is a confident selection of the candidate most likely to succeed in the role.

## Things to be mindful of when hiring for job fit

Hiring for job fit is about finding candidates whose skills, personality, and values align with both the role and the organization. Overlooking key factors can lead to mismatched expectations and higher turnover. Here are some important things to keep in mind when assessing candidates for job fit:

### 1\. Find a balance between hiring for job fit and organizational fit

While assessing for job fit, ensure that you strike a balance between checking how fit the candidate is for a position and how far they can grow within your company. In other words, you must avoid hiring someone who would be an excellent fit for a particular position but a poor fit for any future opportunities.

An example is a software engineer who lacks the communication abilities they’d need to ever step up into a managerial position or client-facing role. To find this balance, you can focus on hiring someone with the technical skills that will help them succeed in the job, and essential soft skills that will take them a long way in your organization.

### 2\. Evaluate candidates’ motivations

Evaluating a candidate’s motivations is an effective way to measure whether or not they’d thrive in your company. Candidates whose motivations and expectations align with what your business offers are more likely to excel in the role.

To evaluate a candidate’s motivations, start by asking structured, open-ended questions during interviews that prompt them to explain what drives their career choices and what excites them in a workplace. Look for consistent themes in their responses, such as a desire for growth, interest in innovation, or preference for stability.

Once you’ve concluded your evaluation, be sure to document these insights in a clear framework so you can compare candidates objectively and identify those with motivations that best suit the role.

### 3\. Optimize your evaluation process.

In a job fit assessment, using conventional evaluation techniques like resume screening or traditional interviews alone doesn’t get you the best results. In fact, in most cases, they hardly tell you enough about your candidate. So, it’s best to combine a skills-based evaluation process with reliable skill tests to hire right.

For effective pre-employment skill testing, using Vervoe lets you assess both technical skills, like HTML proficiency for web developers, and soft skills, like time management, simultaneously and at any scale. Our skill tests come with questions in various formats, like text and audio, letting you assess even the finest details about candidate performance.

Another way to optimize your evaluation process is by integrating structured interviews into your hiring process. This gives you a balance of technical questions that assess hard skills, behavioral and situation-based questions that evaluate soft skills like communication, collaboration, and cultural fit.

## Assess job fit the smart way

Attempting to determine how fit a candidate is for a job through traditional methods, like a resume review, is hard because most candidates tweak their resumes to address each point in the job description.

Similarly, candidates in a job interview situation are likely to stretch the truth and tell you what you want to hear to convince you they’re a good match. Fortunately, with the help of our AI-enhanced skills assessment technology, Vervoe has turned job fit assessment from an art into more of a science.

Incorporating skills assessments into your hiring process will give you confidence that your next hire matches the job fit criteria required to thrive in their position. And, with our candidate scorecards, you get to predict who’s more productive, has high motivation, fits into your work environment, and can successfully handle the tasks of a particular role well.
